insights: All five agents maintain incident closure rates above 91.8%, with the lowest performer (Fred Luddy) resolving 78 of 85 assigned incidents — a performance level that remains operationally robust and outside any threshold for concern.

The closure rate range across agents is only 0.082 and standard deviation is 0.034, indicating negligible performance dispersion — no agent exhibits a statistically or operationally meaningful performance dip.

Fred Luddy handles 92.9% of high-priority incidents and has the longest median resolution time, but this reflects workload composition and complexity rather than performance deficiency, as his closure rate remains above 90%.
No evidence supports a correlation between agent absence and performance degradation, because all agents demonstrate consistently high and stable closure performance across the 13-month period.

The uniformly high performance across all agents — including consistent volume handling, minimal backlog accumulation, and no temporal clustering of unresolved incidents — indicates system-wide operational resilience, not latent vulnerability to absence-driven bottlenecks.
summary: The analysis directly addresses the goal of identifying performance dips tied to a specific agent’s absence, but finds no substantiated evidence of such dips: all five agents sustain closure rates above 91.8%, with extremely low variability (range = 0.082, SD = 0.034). Fred Luddy’s marginally lower rate is fully explained by his disproportionate assignment of high-priority incidents, not capability or availability issues. Consequently, there is no operational justification for absence-related workload redistribution strategies, nor for root-cause investigation into performance decline. The system demonstrates structural stability, and management focus should shift from absence contingency planning to sustaining current performance standards and monitoring for early signs of emerging divergence.
